cuisine: Indian (South Indian style, served in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a paneer entry but no specific entry for fried paneer pepper dish; closest_parent match used at ingredient level.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cheese, fresh (paneer) — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for the primary ingredient; dish-level code not available in FoodEx2 for this preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for fried paneer pepper dish; A0CKP (fresh cheese/paneer) used as primary ingredient anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried paneer pepper dish as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of fried paneer pepper is approximately 250 g, typically served as a main dish or side at an Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) — Paneer and fried paneer preparations; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for spice and oil components
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides nutritional data for paneer and standard Indian paneer preparations. Values for fried paneer pepper are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer data combined with USDA FoodData Central data for black pepper, cooking oil, and spice components. The input record values (energy 192 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 14 g, carbohydrate 6 g per 100 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN paneer fried preparation ranges and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 340 mg/100 g reflects added salt and seasoning consistent with restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on paneer/fresh cheese GI (~27–35) and low-carbohydrate spiced dish profile
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried paneer pepper dish. GI estimated at 35 based on: (1) paneer (fresh cheese) GI of approximately 27–35 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; (2) carbohydrate content is low at 6 g/100 g, predominantly from vegetables and spices; (3) high protein (10 g) and fat (14 g) content further blunts glycemic response. GL per serving = (35 × 6.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.3. This is a conservative upper-bound estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 340 mg/100 g and saturated fat 8.0 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 340 mg/100 g (850 mg per standard 250 g serving) contributes meaningfully toward the 2,000 mg/day hypertension limit; combined with saturated fat at 8.0 g/100 g exceeding the AHA 5 g/100 g threshold for arterial stiffness risk, hypertensive patients should limit portion size to 100–150 g, request reduced salt, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6 g/100 g), high protein (10 g/100 g), estimated GI ~35
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 6 g carbohydrate per 100 g, an estimated GI of 35 (low), and high protein from paneer slowing gastric emptying, this dish has a low glycemic load of approximately 5.3 per standard serving and is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when portion is controlled to 150–200 g and not paired with high-GI carbohydrates such as white rice or naan; monitor saturated fat intake as chronic excess can worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.0 g/100 g and trans fat 0.2 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 8.0 g/100 g (20 g per 250 g serving) substantially exceeds the AHA recommendation of less than 13 g saturated fat per day for a 2,000 kcal diet; cardiovascular disease patients should limit this dish to a 100 g portion, choose pan-frying with minimal oil over deep-frying, and note that the anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, garlic, black pepper) provides partial cardioprotective offset through polyphenol activity.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile vs. elevated saturated fat and nightshade vegetables
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric (curcumin), ginger (gingerols), garlic, and black pepper provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; however, saturated fat at 8.0 g/100 g may promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, and nightshade ingredients (capsicum, green chilli) should be monitored for individual symptom response in RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Paneer Pepper is a South Indian-style dish consisting of bite-sized cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) that are pan-fried or stir-fried and coated in a rich, aromatic black pepper sauce. The dish is seasoned with a blend of anti-inflammatory spices including turmeric, ginger, garlic, curry leaves, and black pepper, and typically includes capsicum and onion for texture and flavour. It is served as a main dish or side at Indian restaurants in Singapore, including North Indian establishments that incorporate South Indian spice techniques. Texturally, the paneer is firm on the outside with a soft, milky interior, and the sauce is bold, peppery, and fragrant. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 10 g protein, 14 g fat (8 g saturated), 6 g carbohydrate, 1.6 g dietary fibre, 340 mg sodium, and 120 mg calcium. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in controlled portions. The dish is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. Clinical considerations include moderate sodium and saturated fat content relevant to hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The strong polyphenol and anti-inflammatory spice profile provides meaningful benefit for inflammatory conditions. It is commonly served with roti, naan, or as a standalone protein-rich snack.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer preparation data and USDA FoodData Central spice/oil components. No exact match for fried paneer pepper dish exists in a single approved source. Values are internally consistent and plausible for the described preparation. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for fried paneer pepper dish not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation not reported in ICMR-NIN; estimated ~200 mg/100 g from paneer and vegetable components but not confirmed by a single approved source entry and therefore set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite fried paneer pepper dish not available as a single approved-source entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.2 g/100 g, a low but non-zero value consistent with trace trans fats from dairy fat in paneer and cooking oil. All core macros (protein_g 10.0, fat_g 14.0, carbohydrate_g 6.0) are non-zero and do not require zero-macro justification. The input record listed two identical portion entries (1 plate, 250 g); deduplicated to one portion in this output. Food group corrected from 'Vegetables' (input) to 'Dairy and eggs' as paneer is the primary ingredient and caloric driver. Cuisine classified as Indian (South Indian style) served in Singapore context.
